detailed_analysis: parse.py is a well-factored Layer-3 adapter slice. The public entry point parse_mcp_server_json (35-60) orchestrates a sequence of small, single-responsibility private helpers (_apply_secret_policy, _extract_canonical, _transport_from_slot, _copy_common_fields, _copy_transport_fields, _copy_stdio_fields, _build_per_tool_views, _tool_only_spellings). No function exceeds 40 lines; the module is 296 lines, under the 300-line ceiling. The secret_policy is applied at the parse egress boundary (line 49) before any canonical is constructed, satisfying NFR-15 and architecture goal 6. Fail-fast is honoured: missing name (93), missing transport (139), missing url (170), missing/empty command (188, 192) all raise ValueError with descriptive messages. The module never references a concrete tool name; agentic_tool_name arrives as a parameter, honouring the stable-abstractions rule and goal 5. The principal residual smells are DRY violations: the canonical field-name vocabulary ('command','args','env','cwd','timeout','headers','auth','disabled','always_allow') is duplicated across _copy_stdio_fields, known_slot_fields, and clear_mcp_fields with no single named source, and these inline string literals are magic strings (I4). Naming is largely strong though a few internal locals ('obj','name','value','headers','extras') are single-word and ambiguous against the project's two-word minimum.

findings.evidence: _copy_stdio_fields uses literals "command","args","env","cwd","timeout" (187-207); known_slot_fields re-lists "command","args","env","cwd","timeout","headers","auth","disabled" (252-276); clear_mcp_fields re-lists "transport","command","args","env","cwd","timeout","url","headers","auth","disabled","always_allow","secret_redactions" (281-294).
findings.problem: The canonical mcp_server field-name set is the single concept that defines what known_slot_fields recognises, what clear_mcp_fields wipes, and what _copy_*_fields populate. It is hand-maintained in three separate literal lists with no shared named constant. Adding or renaming a canonical field requires synchronised edits across all three (a latent Shotgun-Surgery / divergence risk); a missed site silently drops the field into per_agentic_tool_extra or leaves stale state on a prior_canonical. These are also magic strings (I4) with no named constant.

findings.evidence:
name = obj.get(dialect.name_field) or obj.get("name") or canonical.get("name")
...
canonical["pair_id"] = str(pair_id) if pair_id is not None else (canonical.get("pair_id") or new_pair_id())
findings.problem: The hardcoded fallback key "name" (also at line 69) co-exists with dialect.name_field, encoding a dialect assumption inline rather than via the dialect object. The three-term `or` chains plus the nested conditional for pair_id are borderline complex conditionals that mix 'value present in slot' with 'value carried on prior canonical' semantics in one expression.

findings.variant: extract_pair_id may raise on malformed input
findings.problem: Architecture 5.1 rule 3 / US-10 AC requires extract_pair_id to never raise on malformed input and return None instead. This function delegates parsing to loads_slot without a try/except; if loads_slot raises on malformed JSON the contract is broken. The wiring (_mcp_server_factory.extract_pair_id) also does not guard. Confirm loads_slot is itself non-raising on malformed input; if it can raise, this is a contract violation. (Marked INFO pending confirmation of loads_slot behaviour — outside scope.)

findings.evidence: obj = loads_slot(...)  (48); name = obj.get(...) (91); value = obj[always_allow_field] (155); extras = dict(...) (116); headers = headers_from_slot(...) (172)
findings.problem: The project guidelines mandate a two-word minimum for names ('role + domain pair'). Several internal locals use bare nouns ('obj','name','value','extras','headers','result') that do not name what they hold (slot_object, server_name, allow_value, tool_extras). Low severity as scopes are small and types annotate intent, but inconsistent with NFR-14 / naming policy.

verdict_summary: Clean, well-decomposed parse adapter; only DRY/magic-string and naming hygiene issues. Cap at 0.75 on maintainability.
